findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `reviewed_related_material` is threaded from the pre-LLM `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` collection step into `sidecar.emit` / `build_payload` — e.g., identify the exact verdict-dict key under which it should be carried through `_run_plan_review`, or add a `related_material=` parameter to `sidecar.emit` and update every call site.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records already collected before the LLM by child acaf. Serialize reviewed_related_material as an ordered list whose items are exactly {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint}'
Current sidecar.emit signature: `emit(verdict, *, material=None, repo_root=None)` — receives only the post-LLM verdict
Current _run_plan_review call: `sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` at line 608 — no related_material argument
Child acaf collects PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM call in a separate signing path, not in the verdict production path that feeds sidecar.emit
Plan: 'resign_plan_review ... reads reviewed_related_material, and invokes derive_plan_material_pin_health on those records before signing' — this depends on the field existing in the sidecar
findings.finding: The plan extends the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar with `reviewed_related_material` (the pre-LLM `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material`) but does not specify the mechanism for threading this data from its collection point into `sidecar.emit` / `build_payload`. The current `sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` call in `_run_plan_review` only receives the post-LLM verdict and material fingerprint. The `related_material` records are collected before the LLM by child acaf in a separate code path; without an explicit threading mechanism (e.g., embedding under a reserved key in the verdict dict, or adding a `related_material=` parameter to `sidecar.emit`), `resign_plan_review` will not find `reviewed_related_material` in the sidecar payload it reads back, making the sidecar-based re-sign recovery unimplementable as specified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records already collected before the LLM by child acaf'; also sidecar.py `emit` / `build_payload` / `__init__.py` `_run_plan_review`
scenarios: Implementer adds reviewed_related_material to build_payload but has no source to populate it from — the verdict dict has no such field because it was never threaded from the pre-LLM collection step
resign_plan_review reads the sidecar and finds reviewed_related_material absent (treated as legacy-unpinned) even on newly-minted reviews, defeating the sidecar-based re-sign guard
Refresh attestation path (drift_refresh) also calls sidecar.emit but the related_material snapshot is held in the drift_refresh_candidate result which has no related_material field
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `reviewed_related_material: list | None = None` parameter to `sidecar.emit` and `build_payload`, emit it as the serialized `reviewed_related_material` field. In `_run_plan_review`, thread the `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` (obtained from child acaf's collection step) into the `sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, related_material=snapshot.related_material, repo_root=repo_root)` call. For the drift_refresh path, pass `None` (legacy-unpinned behavior) since drift-refresh re-signs without re-running the LLM.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `failure_kind` mapping explicitly: state which exception/error categories (e.g. `FileNotFoundError`/`PermissionError` → `io`, target registry lookup miss → `resolution`, fingerprint hash computation failure → `reducer`) map to each of the three values.
evidence: failure_kind values are enumerated but no mapping from exception/error type to category is given

The AC 'Missing, deleted, unreadable, or reducer-failing targets produce stale-pin-missing; non-missing read failures emit plan_material_pin_target_unreadable without raising' assumes the executor knows the category boundaries
findings.finding: `failure_kind` values are named (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) but the plan never specifies which exception or error types map to each category. An executor implementing `derive_plan_material_pin_health` must decide whether, e.g., a `FileNotFoundError` is `io` or `resolution`, whether a hash-computation error is `reducer` or `io`, and whether a registry lookup failure is `resolution` or `io`. Different choices produce different structured warning payloads and break the AC for `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable`.
findings.location: Implementation section: '`derive_plan_material_pin_health`' bullet — 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` with `failure_kind` exactly `resolution`, `io`, or `reducer`'
scenarios: Executor assigns FileNotFoundError to 'resolution' but reviewer expects 'io', causing divergent structured event payloads
A hash-reducer ValueError is classified as 'reducer' by one implementer, 'io' by another, breaking downstream log parsing
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` bullet: 'Map `failure_kind` as follows: path-not-found or permission errors → `io`; registry/canonical-ID resolution failures (target not in registry) → `resolution`; fingerprint computation or reducer failures → `reducer`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate with the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` spec a dispatch rule that maps specific exception/error types to each `failure_kind` value (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`), e.g. 'resolution = target ID cannot be resolved to a canonical path; io = file read error after resolution; reducer = fingerprint reducer failure'.
evidence: Plan text: 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event plan_material_pin_target_unreadable with failure_kind exactly resolution, io, or reducer; none raises.'
The three values are listed but no mapping from exception types (e.g. FileNotFoundError, OSError, ReducerError) to the three categories is provided
The existing health.py failure_kind pattern in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/health.py does not define the mapping either
ABSENCE: no section in the plan specifies the dispatch rule that routes a caught exception to one of the three failure_kind values
findings.finding: The `failure_kind` three-value vocabulary for the `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` warning event (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) is named only once in a prose sentence. No rule is co-located with the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` spec that maps specific exception or error types to each category. An executor implementing the helper cannot determine which exceptions are `resolution` failures vs. `io` failures vs. `reducer` failures.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` with `failure_kind` exactly `resolution`, `io`, or `reducer`'
scenarios: Two implementors independently pick different mappings: one routes all OSError subclasses to 'io' while another routes FileNotFoundError to 'resolution'; monitoring dashboards see inconsistent failure_kind distributions
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or sentence such as: 'resolution — the target canonical_id cannot be resolved to a store path (missing/deleted); io — the target path resolves but the file read raises OSError or equivalent; reducer — the fingerprint computation raises after a successful read.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the sidecar schema version string remains `plan_review_result_v2` when adding `reviewed_related_material` (preferred: stay on v2, treat new field as a forward-compat optional addition), and assert the post-rollback state — that the rolled-back reader can still consume enriched sidecars via its existing schema guard.
evidence: Plan: 'Rollback removes the additive health key and its consumers; no stored-data migration is required.'
sidecar.py:149-153: schema guard accepts only 'plan_review_result_v1' and 'plan_review_result_v2' — a v3 bump would make post-feature sidecars unreadable on rollback
ABSENCE: the plan never names the schema string that will be emitted for enriched sidecars, nor asserts that no schema bump is needed
resign.py:51: resign_plan_review calls latest_review_result which returns None when all sidecars have an unknown schema — silently blocking sign recovery post-rollback
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the sidecar schema version identifier after adding `reviewed_related_material`, leaving the post-rollback schema state unspecified. The existing `latest_review_result` reader enforces a hard schema allow-list (`plan_review_result_v1` / `plan_review_result_v2`). If the schema is bumped to `v3`, a rollback renders all post-feature sidecars unreadable by the rolled-back reader — `resign_plan_review` would silently return `None` for every ticket reviewed after feature deployment, making sign recovery unavailable without a full re-review. The plan says rollback requires no data migration, but does not assert what schema state is left on disk and whether the old reader can still consume the enriched sidecars.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload' paragraph; AC 'Re-sign preserves the existing own-material staleness guard and never passes a sidecar payload to compute_validity'
scenarios: Feature is deployed, several tickets are reviewed and sidecars written with the new `reviewed_related_material` field under schema `plan_review_result_v3`. A sign failure occurs. Operator rolls back to pre-feature code. The rolled-back `latest_review_result` skips all `v3` sidecars and returns None. `resign_plan_review` returns ok=False with 'no REVIEW_RESULT sidecar found', blocking recovery for every ticket reviewed after deployment — the very scenario resign is designed to handle.
Even if schema is kept at v2, the plan does not explicitly commit to this choice, so an implementer may reasonably bump it, creating the rollback gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep the schema string at `plan_review_result_v2` (the new field is optional/additive and old v2 readers already tolerate unknown keys). Add a plan note: 'The sidecar schema version is not bumped; the enriched payload remains plan_review_result_v2. Post-rollback readers accept these sidecars and ignore the new field, preserving resign_plan_review functionality.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `PlanMaterialPin` explicitly (field names, types, and whether it is a dataclass/TypedDict/namedtuple) and state how `material_fingerprint` from the sidecar maps to `pinned_fingerprint` in `TargetPinDetail` (or rename to be consistent).
evidence: 'derive_plan_material_pin_health(pin_records: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None, ...) ... accepts parsed signed-manifest pins or equivalent review-time sidecar records'
Sidecar items: '{role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint}'
TargetPinDetail uses 'pinned_fingerprint', sidecar uses 'material_fingerprint' — no renaming rule is stated
findings.finding: The `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper signature is declared as `Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None` but the sidecar items it must also accept are serialized as `{role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint}` dicts. The plan never defines `PlanMaterialPin`'s structure, leaving it ambiguous whether `PlanMaterialPin` is a typed dataclass wrapping those fields, a `TypeAlias` for the dict shape, or a pre-existing type. Additionally, `material_fingerprint` (sidecar field name) and `pinned_fingerprint` (`TargetPinDetail` field name) differ — an executor cannot tell if these are the same data under different names or two distinct fields requiring explicit mapping.
findings.location: Implementation section: '`derive_plan_material_pin_health`' bullet and 'Extend the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload' bullet
scenarios: Executor defines PlanMaterialPin with field 'pinned_fingerprint' and the sidecar-reader code silently drops the sidecar's 'material_fingerprint' field, producing malformed-pin for all valid pre-sign sidecars
Executor treats PlanMaterialPin as a plain dict alias and the type checker rejects Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] usage throughout

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `derive_plan_material_pin_health` accepts raw sidecar dicts (update the type annotation to `Sequence[Mapping[str, str]] | None` and document that it parses/validates internally) or accepts pre-parsed `PlanMaterialPin` objects (and describe the explicit parsing/coercion step in the resign.py and sidecar-read paragraphs).
evidence: 'derive_plan_material_pin_health(pin_records: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None, *, repo_root, enforced)'
'On read, `derive_plan_material_pin_health` validates list type, exact item keys, role, canonical ID, fingerprint syntax, order, and duplicate role-plus-ID'
'reads `reviewed_related_material`, and invokes `derive_plan_material_pin_health` on those records'
No described parsing/coercion step between raw sidecar dicts and PlanMaterialPin objects in the resign path
findings.finding: The `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper is typed as accepting `Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None`, but the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar section states the helper itself 'validates list type, exact item keys, role, canonical ID, fingerprint syntax, order, and duplicate role-plus-ID' on read — implying it accepts raw dict records and parses them internally. The resign.py section then passes raw sidecar dicts directly to the helper without a described coercion step. These three sub-sections contradict each other: either the helper accepts already-parsed `PlanMaterialPin` objects (making internal dict-validation impossible) or it accepts raw dicts (making the type annotation wrong). An implementer would not know which responsibility model to implement.
findings.location: Implementation — paragraphs 3 (helper definition), 8 (REVIEW_RESULT sidecar on-read), and 9 (resign.py)
scenarios: Implementer writes the helper to accept typed PlanMaterialPin objects, discovers resign path passes raw dicts, adds ad-hoc coercion outside the helper, losing the validation contract.

Implementer writes the helper to accept raw dicts and validate internally, contradicting the PlanMaterialPin type annotation, and then must decide how compute_validity pre-parses pins before calling the helper.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the helper signature to `derive_plan_material_pin_health(pin_records: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin | Mapping[str, Any]] | None, *, repo_root, enforced)` and explicitly state that raw dict items are validated and coerced internally — or alternatively keep the typed signature and add a `_parse_reviewed_related_material(raw: list) -> Sequence[PlanMaterialPin]` parsing step described in both the sidecar and resign sections, with invalid items producing malformed-pin.

evidence: 29 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the CLOSE/DRIFT_REFRESH AC into two: (a) 'DRIFT_REFRESH disables only code freshness — proved by `test_drift_refresh_candidate.py`' (this story), and (b) 'CLOSE disables only code freshness — proved by child 2bbd's test' (deferred, with explicit rationale), OR add a unit test for the CLOSE profile rule in this story even before its call-site is wired.
evidence: 'child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close' — CLOSE has no wiring step in this story
No verification command or test assertion targets the CLOSE profile
The AC is a single bullet covering two profiles, one of which has no in-story call-site
findings.finding: The acceptance criterion 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH each disable only code freshness in this story and remain separately selected by their owning call sites' is partially unverifiable in-session for this story: `CLOSE` is explicitly not wired in this story ('child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close') and there is no implementation step in this plan that exercises the CLOSE call-site. No test file in the Verification section covers the CLOSE profile's behavior, making the 'CLOSE disables only code freshness' half of this AC untestable within this delivery.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH each disable only code freshness in this story'; Implementation: 'child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close'
scenarios: Agent marks the AC satisfied after only testing DRIFT_REFRESH; CLOSE's freshness-skip behavior is never validated until child 2bbd, at which point this story's completion verdict is already recorded

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for computing health before the reopen/registry/code/material early returns (e.g., 'simultaneous multi-reason reporting allows a single claim-gate call to expose both stale-reopened and pin-drift without a second round-trip') or, if latency is the higher priority, scope health derivation to only the pin-enforcement early return and update the ACs accordingly.
evidence: Plan: 'parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns; every such plan-review return includes health'
The plan states benefit: 'health exposing simultaneous pin drift' — but this is achieved equally by computing health before only the pin-related early return, not before all returns
compute_validity is called on the claim hot path (~50ms target per attest.py doc comment); stale-reopened and stale-regver are the most common early exits on already-invalidated attestations
ABSENCE: plan provides no rationale for why health must be computed before reopen/regver returns (where no pin information is needed)
findings.finding: The plan directs deriving `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` before the reopen, registry, code, and material early returns inside `compute_validity`, which causes target-resolution I/O (fingerprint reads, O(store) lookups per target) to run even when the attestation is already provably invalid for cheaper reasons (e.g., stale-reopened, stale-regver). The plan does not provide a positive rationale for this ordering choice over the alternative of computing health only when all existing guards pass (i.e., only on a candidate-certified result).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'After signature certification and plan-review-kind validation, parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns'
scenarios: A ticket is reopened frequently; every claim-gate check triggers O(N_targets) fingerprint reads even though the reopen check would short-circuit without them

An implementer chooses to compute health only after all existing guards pass (the simpler approach), contradicting the plan's explicit before-returns ordering — creating an ambiguity that yields different observable behavior (simultaneous multi-reason health vs. health-only-on-certified)

findings.finding: The leaf's `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract omits `phase_status` (deferring it to child 5967), but the parent epic specifies the complete nested contract atomically: 'That nested contract has pin_status ... phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail].' The leaf ships a structurally incomplete contract relative to the parent's authoritative specification.
findings.location: Implementation section, second bullet: 'It has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; enforced: bool; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail]'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Map each unmapped AC to a named test file (e.g. add `test_plan_material_pin_health.py` or extend `test_attestation_invalidation.py`) and add those files to the `pytest` verification command.
evidence: Verification command: 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py tests/unit/test_drift_refresh_candidate.py tests/unit/test_plan_material_pin_config.py tests/unit/test_compute_validity_callers.py'
'Archived readable targets compare normally' — no test file named
'Duplicate role-plus-ID pins produce malformed-pin' — no test file named
'Re-enabling enforcement immediately invalidates existing drifted pins' — no test file named
'Malformed manifest or sidecar pin metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' — no test file named
findings.finding: Four acceptance criteria have no named test file or proving command in the Verification section: (1) 'Archived readable targets compare normally', (2) 'Duplicate role-plus-ID pins produce `malformed-pin`', (3) 'Re-enabling enforcement immediately invalidates existing drifted pins', and (4) 'Malformed manifest or sidecar pin metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising'. None of these are covered by any of the six named pytest files in the Verification block.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (four listed ACs) and Verification section (pytest command lists six test files)
scenarios: All six test files pass but archived-target comparison, duplicate-pin detection, re-enable invalidation, and malformed-input handling are never exercised, so the implementation may be wrong for these cases at completion sign-off
A completion verdict is issued while these four behaviors are untested

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE can be deferred to child 2bbd (where it gains its first call-site), shipping only DEFAULT and DRIFT_REFRESH here, or add a wired CLOSE call-site to this story's scope so the AC is verifiable at ship.
evidence: Plan: 'child 2bbd later wires CLOSE into transition_close'
Plan: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story—disable only code freshness'
No existing codebase symbol PlanValidityProfile (grep returns zero hits in src/rebar)
AC requires both values be 'separately selected by their owning call sites' but CLOSE has no call site at ship
findings.finding: PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE ships with zero wired call-sites: its only named consumer (transition_close) is explicitly deferred to child 2bbd. An enum value with no active call-site at ship is premature abstraction; it adds API surface and test-contract obligations (AC: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH each separately selected by their owning call sites') that cannot be verified at this story's boundary.
findings.location: Implementation § PlanValidityProfile / AC: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH each disable only code freshness in this story and remain separately selected by their owning call sites'
scenarios: CLOSE ships as dead code; the AC asserting it is 'separately selected' cannot be green-tested at this story's boundary.
If child 2bbd changes close semantics before wiring, CLOSE's meaning silently diverges from its definition here with no enforcement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify `PlanValidityProfile` as a named enum (vs a simpler `skip_code_freshness: bool` kwarg or `frozenset` of disabled checks), or note which child story is expected to diverge CLOSE from DRIFT_REFRESH behavior first, satisfying the Rule-of-Three before committing to the enum shape.
evidence: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story—disable only code freshness—but remain distinct call-site intents so later close policy cannot silently change progressive refresh'
ABSENCE: No comparison to simpler alternatives (skip_code_freshness kwarg, frozenset of disabled checks) is made
Only two non-DEFAULT values today, both with identical runtime behavior — the Rule-of-Three is not yet satisfied
findings.finding: PlanValidityProfile introduces a THREE-value enum (DEFAULT / CLOSE / DRIFT_REFRESH) where CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH are behaviorally identical today ('disable only code freshness'). The plan justifies the split as a forward-looking anti-conflation guard, but does not compare it against a simpler established pattern (e.g., a single `skip_code_freshness: bool` kwarg, or a frozenset of disabled check names) that would achieve the same call-site separation with less novel indirection. Two callers with identical behavior today is below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a dedicated enum.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'Define PlanValidityProfile with exactly DEFAULT, CLOSE, and DRIFT_REFRESH...' and 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story—disable only code freshness—but remain distinct call-site intents'
scenarios: A reviewer implementing this story must add a three-branch enum dispatch with two branches doing the same thing; future maintainers may add profile-specific logic to one branch inconsistently (e.g. adding a CLOSE-only reopen bypass that should have been in DRIFT_REFRESH too)
If child 5967 never adds phase compatibility divergence between CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH, the enum permanently has two identical cases
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the PlanValidityProfile definition explaining the first expected behavioral divergence between CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH (e.g., 'child 5967 adds phase-compatibility enforcement to CLOSE but not DRIFT_REFRESH, satisfying the Rule-of-Three'). Alternatively, use `skip_code_freshness: bool = False` on `compute_validity` until the divergence materializes.

grep of src/ for 'PlanRelationSnapshot' returns zero matches

AC: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH each disable only code freshness in this story and remain separately selected by their owning call sites' — confirms both are active enum values but gives no branching spec
findings.finding: `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` and `PlanValidityProfile.DRIFT_REFRESH` are described with identical behavioral rules in this story ('CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story—disable only code freshness') but no decision rule is co-located with the `compute_validity` signature that tells an executor how to branch on the profile parameter. The plan says 'keep distinct call-site intents' as the only differentiation rationale but provides no branching logic specification: an executor writing the `profile` parameter dispatch in `compute_validity` has no rule to implement for the CLOSE case.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Define `PlanValidityProfile`...' and AC line 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH each disable only code freshness in this story'
scenarios: Executor implements compute_validity with `if profile in (CLOSE, DRIFT_REFRESH): skip_code_freshness = True` which is the only reasonable inference — but if child 2bbd later expects CLOSE to behave differently from DRIFT_REFRESH at the compute_validity level, this story's implementation will need revision
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small table: DEFAULT: all existing checks active. CLOSE: reopen + signature + registry + own-material + pin checks active; code-freshness (stale-code / stale-head) skipped. DRIFT_REFRESH: same skips as CLOSE.

findings.finding: The AC 'No warning event is persisted in the ticket event store for derived health; advisory state is exposed through the returned health contract and child cb6b surfaces' has no associated test file, pytest command, or proving mechanism in the Verification section. It is a negative runtime assertion about the ticket event store with no in-session check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'No warning event is persisted in the ticket event store for derived health'
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two ACs: (1) 'derive_plan_material_pin_health never calls the ticket event writer; verified by asserting no call to <event_writer_function> in test_attestation_invalidation.py or test_plan_material_pin_config.py'; (2) '[deferred to child cb6b] Advisory health state is surfaced in audit/UI rendering.'

findings.finding: The plan defines `compute_validity` as the single extended function (with `profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT`), but child 2bbd (a direct dependent of this story) references `compute_plan_review_validity(profile=close)` — a different function name. This interface mismatch between what this plan produces and what child 2bbd consumes means a conforming implementation of both stories will have a broken call site in `transition_close`.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT)'; child 2bbd description: 'call compute_plan_review_validity(profile=close)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line alias `compute_plan_review_validity = compute_validity` to `attest.py`'s public exports, or update child 2bbd's spec to call `compute_validity` directly with `profile=PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE`. The alias approach is lower blast-radius and can be removed once 2bbd is implemented.

findings.finding: The leaf keeps the validity function named `compute_validity` and explicitly says 'without renaming or wrapping it', but the parent epic names the unified boundary `compute_plan_review_validity` ('compute_plan_review_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth'). The leaf contradicts the parent's specified symbol name for the shared contract.
findings.location: Implementation section, fifth bullet: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(...) without renaming or wrapping it'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/golden/config_surface.json` explicitly to the plan's edit-target list and note that it must be regenerated after adding `enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS["verify"]`.
evidence: tests/golden/config_surface.json currently has no 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins' entry

tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py line 9: 'It intentionally does NOT fail when a NEW live key is absent from the golden (adding a surface is not a regression); regenerate the golden deliberately when you add one.'
Plan states 'the config-surface golden records the key' without naming the golden file as an explicit edit target
findings.finding: `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is an implicit edit target that the plan requires updating but does not name explicitly. The plan states 'the config-surface golden records the key' after adding `enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS['verify']`, but `tests/golden/config_surface.json` (the file at `tests/golden/config_surface.json`) must be manually regenerated/updated to add the key. An implementer following only the named edit targets will miss this step, causing `test_plan_material_pin_config.py` to pass but leaving the golden stale (though `test_config_surface_golden.py` won't fail on an *added* key — only on removed ones — so the gap is silent).
findings.location: Implementation section, first bullet: '…the config-surface golden records the key'
scenarios: Implementer adds enforce_plan_material_pins to _SECTIONS['verify'] and VerifyConfig but does not regenerate tests/golden/config_surface.json — the key is live but the anti-regression golden is stale, silently defeating its tracking purpose.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Regenerate `tests/golden/config_surface.json` (run the golden-update script) to record `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` in the anti-regression surface.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `tests/golden/config_surface.json` must be explicitly regenerated (e.g., `python scripts/regenerate_config_golden.py` or equivalent) after adding `enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS['verify']`, since the golden test only checks that keys previously in the golden remain live (it does NOT fail on a new live key absent from the golden).
evidence: tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py:9 explicitly states 'It intentionally does NOT fail when a NEW live key is absent from the golden'
tests/golden/config_surface.json does not contain 'enforce_plan_material_pins' (confirmed by search)
test_no_config_key_removed_without_a_tombstone checks golden ⊆ live, not live ⊆ golden
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the config-surface golden records the key' after adding `enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS['verify']`, but `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is a manually-maintained golden file that does NOT auto-update when a new key is added. `test_config_surface_golden.py` only checks that GOLDEN-pinned keys still exist in the live schema (anti-removal guard) — it does NOT fail when a new live key is absent from the golden. The author must explicitly regenerate the golden file after adding the key.
findings.location: Implementation section, first bullet: 'the config-surface golden records the key'
scenarios: If the implementer adds `enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS` but does not regenerate the golden, the test suite passes (the test checks the opposite direction), giving a false impression the golden was updated.
The AC 'the config-surface golden records the key' will be trivially unverifiable unless the implementer also updates the golden file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit step in the plan: 'After registering the key in `_SECTIONS["verify"]`, regenerate `tests/golden/config_surface.json` by running `<the regeneration command>` and commit the updated golden file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the extension protocol for `DerivedPlanReviewHealth`: declare all child-story-added fields as optional with defaults in this story's definition, specify the sequencing constraint (5967 before cb6b), or define a versioned/tagged contract so existing consumers remain valid as children extend it.
evidence: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with `phase_status` and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format'
'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings'
No versioning, optional-field tagging, or extension protocol is specified for `DerivedPlanReviewHealth`
Multiple consumers named: `claim_gate_check`, `drift_refresh_candidate`, `resign_plan_review`, child cb6b renderers
findings.finding: The `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract is described as extensible by child stories (5967 adds `phase_status`, cb6b owns audit/UI rendering) without specifying an extension mechanism — no versioning, no optional-field protocol, and no interface boundary between this story's definition and children's additions — leaving all current consumers at breakage risk when a child story extends or modifies the contract.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with `phase_status` and phase compatibility'; 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings'
scenarios: Child 5967 adds `phase_status` as a required field, breaking any consumer that constructs or pattern-matches the contract shape before 5967 lands
Child cb6b's rendering code is written against the current contract shape, then breaks when 5967 extends it
The extension ordering between 5967 and cb6b is not specified, so one child silently assumes the other has already extended the contract
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `phase_status` and any other child-story fields as `Optional` with a `None` default in the base `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` definition in this story, so child stories populate them without breaking current consumers. Document the sequencing dependency (cb6b rendering should depend on 5967's extension landing first).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `PlanMaterialPin` field schema (required field names, types, and which missing/invalid field values constitute 'malformed record syntax') co-located with the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper spec.
evidence: Plan says: 'It accepts parsed signed-manifest pins or equivalent review-time sidecar records' and 'Malformed record syntax is data, not an exception at these consumer boundaries'
Plan says: 'parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth' without defining what parsed pins look like
grep of src/ for 'PlanMaterialPin' returns zero matches — type does not exist yet
The sidecar section defines `reviewed_related_material` items as `{role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint}` but the signed-manifest pin format is never specified
ABSENCE: no section defines PlanMaterialPin's fields, required vs optional keys, or type constraints
findings.finding: `PlanMaterialPin` is the typed input to `derive_plan_material_pin_health(pin_records: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None, ...)` — the central new helper — but its field structure (field names, types, what constitutes 'malformed record syntax') is never defined anywhere in this plan. An executor implementing the helper cannot determine what fields to read, which fields constitute a valid record vs. a malformed one, or how to deserialize entries from the signed manifest.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Add one shared `derive_plan_material_pin_health`...'
scenarios: Executor writes derive_plan_material_pin_health but cannot determine the field name for the fingerprint in a manifest pin (is it 'pinned_fingerprint', 'material_fingerprint', or something else?)
Malformed-pin detection is ambiguous — no field schema means no way to know what 'malformed' means for manifest pins vs sidecar records

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing: (a) the per-key fail-open config-reading pattern and its intentional divergence from general `verify.*` posture, (b) the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract and its extension protocol for child stories, and (c) the rationale for three `PlanValidityProfile` variants when `CLOSE` and `DRIFT_REFRESH` currently share one rule.
evidence: 'intentional key-local fail-open rollout policy modeled on `mcp_readonly()`, not the general `verify.*` posture'
'Own the nested `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract returned as `health` beside the existing attest.compute_validity top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
'Define `PlanValidityProfile` with exactly `DEFAULT`, `CLOSE`, and `DRIFT_REFRESH`'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` cross-cutting contract, the `PlanValidityProfile` enum, the per-key fail-open config-reading pattern (`_read_enforce_plan_material_pins`) that deliberately diverges from the general `verify.*` posture, and pin enforcement as a distinct validity dimension — without specifying any update to an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design document.
findings.location: Implementation section (DerivedPlanReviewHealth, PlanValidityProfile, _read_enforce_plan_material_pins descriptions)
scenarios: A future maintainer encounters the per-key fail-open pattern, doesn't know it's intentional, and 'fixes' it to match the general verify posture, silently breaking the rollout policy
A child story author doesn't know the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` extension protocol and makes incompatible changes
An onboarding engineer cannot discover why `PlanValidityProfile` has three values when only two behaviors exist in this story

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a short-circuit escape hatch (e.g., a separate boolean config flag or a trivially-toggled code path) so the `drift_refresh_candidate` delegation to `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)` can be reverted without a full redeploy if the new delegation proves defective.
evidence: 'Remove the redundant inline signature, kind, registry, and own-material guards'
'If DRIFT_REFRESH delegation is defective, restore the removed inline signature/kind/registry/own-material guards from version control; this also requires no data migration'
No flag or toggle is described that can reactivate the removed guards without a code deploy
findings.finding: The `drift_refresh_candidate` structural refactor (removing inline signature, kind, registry, and own-material guards and delegating to `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)`) has no flag-based rollback path — only a full code revert. The `enforce_plan_material_pins` flag cannot recover this if the DRIFT_REFRESH delegation is subtly wrong.
findings.location: Implementation § drift_refresh_candidate; Rollback note: 'If DRIFT_REFRESH delegation is defective, restore the removed inline signature/kind/registry/own-material guards from version control'
scenarios: DRIFT_REFRESH profile under compute_validity has a bug that allows invalid attestations to pass as progressive-refresh candidates — the only recovery is a code deploy, not a config toggle, potentially leaving a window where bad attestations are refreshed
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a `use_compute_validity_for_drift_refresh: bool = True` config key (default true) in `_config_schema.py` with the same fail-open pattern as `enforce_plan_material_pins`; when false, `drift_refresh_candidate` falls back to its original inline guards. Remove once the delegation is proven stable in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an aggregate observable signal (e.g., a counter metric or a single structured log event at `WARNING` or `ERROR` level) emitted when `derive_plan_material_pin_health` encounters any non-absence failure, so operators can detect systematic target-resolution breakage even in advisory mode.
evidence: 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event plan_material_pin_target_unreadable with failure_kind exactly resolution, io, or reducer; none raises'
'When disabled, report the same observations as advisory while valid remains true'
No aggregate counter, metric, or alert is specified for repeated or widespread target-resolution failures
findings.finding: Silent systematic target-resolution failures in advisory mode (enforcement disabled) produce no observable signal beyond per-target `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` log warnings; there is no aggregate metric or alert to detect fleet-wide or persistent pin-health degradation when it would silently not affect `valid`.
findings.location: Implementation § derive_plan_material_pin_health; AC 'Non-missing read failures emit plan_material_pin_target_unreadable without raising'
scenarios: All target fingerprint resolutions start failing (e.g., a shared utility function is broken) while enforcement is disabled; every attestation check silently produces stale-pin-missing advisory health with valid=true and no operator notices until enforcement is enabled
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a single `plan_material_pin_health_degraded` structured log event at `ERROR` level whenever `derive_plan_material_pin_health` produces an aggregate status of `stale-pin-missing` due to a read/resolution/reducer failure (as opposed to a genuinely deleted target), regardless of enforcement mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the dedicated _read_enforce_plan_material_pins boundary function with concrete divergence risk or ≥3 call-sites, OR simplify to a direct load_config().verify.enforce_plan_material_pins read at each consumer (matching the pattern used by the rest of VerifyConfig's bool keys).
evidence: mcp_readonly() justification: 'Both read-only call sites route through this — the MCP server's write-tool gating and the LLM runner's comment-tool withholding — so the two cannot diverge (they once did)' (src/rebar/config.py:402-414)
Plan names only 2 consumers of enforce_plan_material_pins: compute_validity and resign_plan_review (both in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/)
No prior divergence or separate-module call-site risk cited for this new key
findings.finding: _read_enforce_plan_material_pins is introduced as a dedicated reader-boundary function modeled on mcp_readonly(), but the mcp_readonly() pattern is justified by two physically separate, historically divergent call sites (mcp_server and runner). The new key's consumers (compute_validity and resign_plan_review) are adjacent modules in the same plan_review cluster with no demonstrated divergence history, so the dedicated boundary function is cargo-culting the mcp_readonly pattern without the same Rule-of-Three / demonstrated-divergence justification.
findings.location: Implementation § _read_enforce_plan_material_pins / 'the only read boundary for this optional key... modeled on mcp_readonly()'
scenarios: A thin wrapper around load_config().verify.enforce_plan_material_pins at 2 adjacent call sites achieves the same effect without the extra indirection layer.
The dedicated boundary adds a test-contract obligation (test_plan_material_pin_config.py) for a pattern that isn't yet justified by call-site count.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite declaration in the plan that this story depends on child `acaf` for `PlanMaterialPin`, `PlanRelationSnapshot`, and their associated helpers, since none of these types exist in `src/` today.
evidence: Search for 'PlanMaterialPin' in src/ returns zero results
Search for 'PlanRelationSnapshot' in src/ returns zero results
Search for 'reviewed_related_material' in src/ returns zero results
Search for 'derive_plan_material_pin_health' in src/ returns zero results

.rebar/cache/plan-review/75de-02b0-d454-410b/d7391915fe588d9b2d94.json confirms this gap: 'The plan asserts PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshot, reviewed_related_material, and derive_plan_material_pin_health are available inputs — but all are deliverables of sibling story acaf'
findings.finding: `PlanMaterialPin`, `PlanRelationSnapshot`, `reviewed_related_material`, and `derive_plan_material_pin_health` — all named as available inputs/helpers in this plan's Implementation — do not exist anywhere in the codebase (`src/`). They are deliverables of sibling story `acaf`, not yet implemented. The plan uses them as if they are already present without an explicit declared prerequisite on `acaf`.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add one shared `derive_plan_material_pin_health(pin_records: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None, ...)` helper'; 'Extend the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload with the exact review-time `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` records already collected before the LLM by child acaf'
scenarios: An implementer reading this plan may not realize that the typed inputs for `derive_plan_material_pin_health` do not yet exist and must be imported from / co-defined with story acaf.
If story acaf's types are not yet merged, this story cannot be implemented without forward-porting the type definitions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` as an explicit edit target and describe how the pre-LLM `related_material` snapshot (from child acaf's collection step) is threaded through to the `sidecar.emit(...)` call at line 608.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:608: `sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` — does not currently pass any related_material argument
Plan does not list plan_review/__init__.py as an edit target
sidecar.emit/build_payload confirmed at sidecar.py:51/60 — accepts verdict + material only
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` is an unenumerated edit target. The plan extends the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload with `reviewed_related_material`, but the only production caller of `sidecar.emit` for plan-review results is `__init__.py:608` (`sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)`). For `reviewed_related_material` to be serialized into the sidecar, this call site must be updated to pass the pre-LLM related_material snapshot that child acaf collects. The plan names `sidecar.py` and `resign.py` as edit targets but not `__init__.py`.
findings.location: Implementation section, bullet: 'Extend the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar payload with the exact review-time `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` records already collected before the LLM by child acaf.'
scenarios: Implementer extends sidecar.build_payload to accept reviewed_related_material but does not update the __init__.py:608 call site — the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar is emitted without the new field, resign.py reads an absent field, and all attestations behave as legacy-unpinned regardless of enforcement setting.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the full aggregation decision rule (severity chain, tie-breaking, and the `legacy-unpinned` boundary condition) with the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` helper spec rather than only in the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` schema paragraph.
evidence: Plan text: 'Aggregate target status by fixed severity malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current; legacy-unpinned occurs only for a wholly unpinned manifest with an empty targets list.'
The aggregation rule appears in the schema description paragraph, not in the derive_plan_material_pin_health helper spec where it is actually applied

The `current` value appears in TargetPinDetail.pin_status vocabulary but NOT in DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status vocabulary (which has 5 values including legacy-unpinned and malformed-pin); the aggregation rule omits 'legacy-unpinned' from the severity chain, leaving ambiguous what the aggregate is when a mix of current and legacy-unpinned per-target states appear
findings.finding: The severity-ordering aggregation rule that reduces the per-`TargetPinDetail` list into the single top-level `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` is stated in one prose sentence ('Aggregate target status by fixed severity malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current') but is NOT co-located with either the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` schema definition or the `TargetPinDetail` schema definition. When `legacy-unpinned` can co-exist with target entries and what `legacy-unpinned` means as an aggregate output is also only implied, not stated as a formal rule.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Own the nested `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract...'
scenarios: An executor implementing derive_plan_material_pin_health applies the severity order at the wrong layer (per-target instead of aggregate) or in the wrong function
Edge case: one target is current and another has legacy-unpinned pin_status — the severity ordering chain does not include legacy-unpinned so the aggregate result is undefined

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
